parsed.summary: The v0.4.x cumulative diff introduces retry/backoff logic, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, and significant state management changes. While there are accompanying tests, several files show patterns that could cause silent data loss, stuck jobs, or corrupted state under partial failure. The retry module and job tracking logic are the highest-risk areas.

findings.title: retry.mjs exponential backoff may silently swallow retried operations that partially succeed
findings.body: The retry module (203 lines added) implements exponential backoff with jitter. If the wrapped operation has side effects (e.g., writing state, creating jobs) and fails after partial completion, retries will re-execute the entire operation without idempotency guards. This can cause duplicate state entries or double-processed jobs. The risk is elevated because retry is used by glm-client.mjs (155 lines changed) which manages job submission to BigModel — a network operation that can fail after the server has already accepted the request.
findings.recommendation: Audit every call site of retry.mjs to confirm each operation is idempotent. For glm-client.mjs job submission, add a client-generated idempotency key that survives retries so the server can deduplicate. Add a test case where the operation succeeds on the server but the response is lost, then verify retry does not create a duplicate job.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs state corruption risk on concurrent access
findings.body: The tracked-jobs module (78 lines changed) manages job state presumably via filesystem. If two glm-companion processes run concurrently (e.g., two terminal sessions), they can race on the same state file, leading to lost job entries or corrupted JSON. The state.mjs module (32 lines changed) likely uses atomic-ish file writes, but without file locking, concurrent writers will interleave reads and writes under load.
findings.recommendation: Implement advisory file locking (e.g., lockfile with stale-lock detection) around state file reads and writes. Alternatively, use atomic rename (write to temp file, then rename) for writes and add a sequence number to detect lost updates. Add a test that simulates concurrent write-modify-write and asserts no data is lost.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs error classification may misclassify unknown errors as retryable
findings.body: The BigModel error dispatch (168 lines added) maps error codes to retry/recovery strategies. If BigModel introduces a new error code that should be fatal (e.g., account suspended, content policy violation), the default classification may treat it as transient and retry indefinitely until max retries exhaust. This wastes API quota and delays user notification of a permanent failure. The retry.test.mjs (352 lines) should include coverage for unknown error codes but the risk remains if the default branch is overly optimistic.
findings.recommendation: Make the default error classification conservative — treat unknown errors as non-retryable (fail fast) rather than retryable. Log a warning with the unclassified error code so it can be triaged. Add a test that verifies an unrecognized error code is classified as non-retryable.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs lacks response validation before marking job as successful
findings.body: The glm-client module (155 lines changed) submits jobs to BigModel and processes responses. If the API returns a 200 with a malformed or incomplete response body (e.g., missing job ID, empty result), the client may record the job as completed with garbage data. There is no evidence of schema validation on the API response before state transitions. This would cause downstream consumers of the job result to crash or produce incorrect output silently.
findings.recommendation: Add response body schema validation (even a shallow check for required fields like job ID and status) before transitioning job state to 'completed'. If validation fails, treat the response as a transient error and retry rather than recording corrupt state.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted without clear migration — potential import breakage
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs (67 lines removed entirely) was deleted in this diff. If any other module imported from process.mjs (even indirectly through re-exports or dynamic imports), those imports will fail at runtime with MODULE_NOT_FOUND. The deletion is especially risky because the module was a library module (under lib/), suggesting it may have had consumers beyond the explicitly changed files.
findings.recommendation: Run a full import graph analysis (the check-imports.mjs script exists but verify it covers dynamic imports). Grep the entire codebase for 'process.mjs' or any exported symbol from that module. If any consumer remains, either restore the module with a deprecation shim or migrate the consumer before deleting.

findings.title: configure-self-hosted forge-protection.sh shell script lacks error handling for partial API failures
findings.body: The self-hosted forge branch protection setup script (126 lines added) makes multiple sequential API calls to configure branch protection. If an intermediate call fails (e.g., network timeout, permission error), the script continues configuring subsequent rules, leaving the repository in a partially protected state. This is a security configuration — partial application is worse than no application because it gives a false sense of protection.
findings.recommendation: Add 'set -euo pipefail' at the top of the script. Wrap each API call with error checking and exit on failure with a descriptive message. Consider making the script idempotent so it can be re-run to fix partial state.

next_steps: Inspect retry.mjs call sites to audit idempotency of each wrapped operation
Verify tracked-jobs.mjs uses atomic writes and test concurrent access scenarios
Run full import graph check to confirm process.mjs deletion has no dangling consumers
Check bigmodel-errors.mjs default error classification strategy — ensure unknown errors fail fast
Review glm-client.mjs response handling for schema validation before state transitions
Run the existing test suite against the diff to identify any failing tests before merge
